volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Comedy of Asses (Section 1.1.105b - Section 1.3.232)

sections.label: Section 1.1.105b
sections.original: Si forte in insidias devenero ,
sections.translation: if the foe should intercept me , will you ransom me ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.107
sections.original: Redimam .
sections.translation: I will ransom you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.107b
sections.original: Tum tu igitur aliud cura quid lubet .
sections.translation: Then do you attend to something else , whatever you please .

sections.label: Section 1.1.108
sections.original: ego eo ad forum , nisi quid vis .
sections.translation: I ’ m off to the Forum , unless you wish for anything .

sections.label: Section 1.1.108b
sections.original: Ei , bene ambula .
sections.translation: Be off — why are you not walking [*] ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.109
sections.original: atque audin etiam ?
sections.translation: And do you hear , too —?

sections.label: Section 1.1.109b
sections.original: Ecce .
sections.translation: Well now .

sections.label: Section 1.1.109c
sections.original: Si quid te volam ,
sections.translation: If I want you for anything , where will you be ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.110b
sections.original: Vbicumque libitum erit animo meo .
sections.translation: Wherever it shall be agreable to my feelings . Really , there ’ s not a person that I shall stand in dread of from this time forward , for fear he might be able to do me an injury , since in your discourse you have disclosed to me all your sentiments . Why , your own self even I don ’ t stand much in awe of , if I carry this out . I ’ ll go where I intended , and there I ’ ll commune upon my plans .

sections.label: Section 1.1.116
sections.original: Audin tu ? apud Archibulum ego ero argentarium .
sections.translation: Do you hear me ? I shall be with Archibulus , the banker .

sections.label: Section 1.1.117
sections.original: Nempe in foro ?
sections.translation: In the Forum , you mean ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.117b
sections.original: Ibi , si quid opus fuerit .
sections.translation: There , if there shall be any occasion for me [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.117c
sections.original: Meminero .—
sections.translation: I ’ ll remember it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.118
sections.original: Non esse servos peior hoc quisquam potest
sections.translation: Not any servant can there be more artful than this fellow , nor yet more crafty , nor one that it is more difficult for you to be on your guard against .

sections.label: Section 1.1.120
sections.original: eidem homini , si quid recte curatum velis ,
sections.translation: If you want anything well managed , entrust it to this same fellow ; he ’ d rather he should die in wretchedness , than not have that quite completed which he has promised . For I know that this money is as surely forthcoming for my son , as that I look upon this same walking-stick .

sections.label: Section 1.1.125
sections.original: sed quid ego cesso ire ad forum , quo inceperam ?
sections.translation: But why am I delaying to go to the Forum where I had intended ⋯

sections.label: Section 1.2.127
sections.original: Sícine hoc fít ? foras aédibus me éici ?
sections.translation: Is ’ t thus it is ,— me to be shut out of doors ? Is this the reward that ’ s given to me who deserve so highly of you ? To him who deserves well you are unkind , to him who deserves ill you are indulgent .

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.original: at malo cum tuo , nam iam ex hoc loco
sections.translation: But to your own misfortune , for now from this spot will I go to the Triumvirs [*] , and there I ’ ll take care your names shall be . I ’ ll punish capitally yourself and your daughter , you enticers , pests , and destruction of young men ! For , compared with you , the sea is not the sea ; you are a most dangerous sea .

sections.label: Section 1.2.135
sections.original: nam in mari repperi , hic elavi bonis .
sections.translation: For on the sea did I find it , here have I been cleaned out of my wealth . What I have given , and what kindnesses I have done , I find them all valueless for good , and thrown away . But from hence-forth , whatever harm I shall be able to do you , I will do it , and do it at your deserts . I ’ faith , I ’ ll reduce you to the verge of poverty , that state from which you have risen .

sections.label: Section 1.2.140
sections.original: ego edepol te faciam ut quae sis nunc et quae fueris scias .
sections.translation: By my troth , I ’ ll make you to know what you now are , and what you once were ; what you were before I visited that daughter of yours , and , in my passion , bestowed upon her my affection ; on coarse bread [*] you were enjoying your life , in rags , and in want . And if these you had , especial thanks did you return to all the Divinities . Now , bad woman , you the same person , when ’ tis better with you , don ’ t know me through whose means it is so .

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: reddam ego te ex fera fame mansuetem , me specta modo .
sections.translation: From a wild beast , I ’ ll make you tame through hunger , only trust me for that [*] . But I have no reason to blame your daughter herself ; she does not deserve it in the least . She acts by your command ; she obeys your bidding ; you are her mother , you too her mistress . I ’ ll revenge myself on you ; I ’ ll ruin you ; as you are deserving , and as you merit at my hands . But look now , the hag , how she really doesn ’ t think me worthy

sections.label: Section 1.2.150
sections.original: quém adeat , quem conloquatur quoique irato supplicet ?
sections.translation: for her to come to and address and deprecate my resentment . But , see , the enticer ’ s coming out at last , I think . Here . before the door , I ’ ll address her in my own fashion , as I please , since I ’ m not allowed to do so within .

sections.label: Section 1.3.153
sections.original: Vnum quodque istorum verbum nummis Philippis aureis
sections.translation: If any purchaser should come , he could not carry him away from me each single word of those for gold Philippean pieces .

sections.label: Section 1.3.155
sections.original: nec recte quae tu in nos dicis , aurum atque argentum merumst :
sections.translation: What you say wrongfully against us is good gold and silver . Your heart is locked up here with us , at the helm of Cupid . With oars and with sails make haste and fly as fast as you can ; the further you betake yourself to sea , the more the tide will bring you back to harbour .

sections.label: Section 1.3.159
sections.original: Ego pol istum portitorem privabo portorio ;
sections.translation: I faith , I ’ ll be depriving this custom-house officer [*] of his dues .

sections.label: Section 1.3.160
sections.original: ego te dehinc ut merita es de me et mea re tractare exsequar ,
sections.translation: From henceforth I ’ ll persist in treating you as you have deserved of me and mine , since you have treated me not as I deserve , in excluding me from your house .

sections.label: Section 1.3.162
sections.original: Magis istuc percipimus lingua dici , quam factis fore .
sections.translation: I know that that is rather said with the tongue , than that it will happen in deed .

sections.label: Section 1.3.163
sections.original: Solus solitudine ego ted atque ab egestate abstuli ;
sections.translation: I alone have brought you from obscurity and from want ; if I alone patronize you , you can never return sufficient thanks .

sections.label: Section 1.3.165
sections.original: Solus ductato , si semper solus quae poscam dabis ;
sections.translation: Do you still be the only one to patronize me , if you alone will always give me what I ask . Do you always keep what has been promised you , on this condition , that you surpass others in your presents .

sections.label: Section 1.3.167
sections.original: Qui modus dandi ? nam numquam tu quidem expleri potes ;
sections.translation: What limit is there to be to giving ? For really you can never be satisfied ; the moment that you ’ ve received something , not very long after , you are devising something for you to be asking for .

sections.label: Section 1.3.169
sections.original: Quid modist ductando , amando ? numquamne expleri potes ?
sections.translation: What limit is there to be to your enjoying yourself , and to your indulging your amour ? Can you never be satisfied ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.170
sections.original: modo remisisti , continuo iam ut remittam ad te rogas .
sections.translation: The moment that you have sent her home , that instant , you are directly asking me to send her back to you .

sections.label: Section 1.3.171
sections.original: Dedi equidem quod mecum egisti .
sections.translation: In fact , I have given whatever you have demanded of me .

sections.label: Section 1.3.171b
sections.original: Et tibi ego misi mulierem :
sections.translation: And I have sent the damsel to you . A requital has been given , like for like ; a return for the money .

sections.label: Section 1.3.173
sections.original: Male agis mecum .
sections.translation: You treat me badly .

sections.label: Section 1.3.173b
sections.original: Quid me accusas , si facio officium meum ?
sections.translation: Why do you blame me if I do my duty ? For nowhere is it either feigned in story or represented in pictures or written in poems ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.175
sections.original: ubi lena bene agat cum quiquam amante , quae frugi esse volt .
sections.translation: where a procuress , who wishes to thrive , treats any lover well .

sections.label: Section 1.3.176
sections.original: Mihi quidem te parcere aequomst tandem , ut tibi durem diu .
sections.translation: Still , ’ twere right for you to show favour to me at least , that I might last the longer for you .

sections.label: Section 1.3.177
sections.original: Non tu scis ? quae amanti parcet , eadem sibi parcet parum .
sections.translation: Don ’ t you know , the woman that shows favour to a lover , that same woman shows little favour to herself ? Just like a fish , so is a lover to a procuress ; he ’ s good for nothing if he isn ’ t fresh . Then it has juice , then it has sweetness ; in any fashion you like you may season it ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.180
sections.original: vel patinarium vel assum , verses quo pacto lubet :
sections.translation: either stewed or roasted ; ill any way you will , you may turn it . So the lover ; he ’ s ready to give , he longs for something to be asked of him , for there it ’ s taken from a full stock , nor does he know what he ’ s giving , or what mischief he ’ s doing . Of this matter does the new lover think ; he wishes himself to please his mistress , he wishes to please me , he wishes to please her lady ’ s maid [*] , he wishes to please the men-servants , he wishes to please the maid-servants as well , and even my dog

sections.label: Section 1.3.185
sections.original: subblanditur novos amator , se ut quom videat gaudeat .
sections.translation: does he caress , that when it sees him , it may be delighted . I tell the truth ; it shows cleverness for every person to be fair-dealing for his own advantage .

sections.label: Section 1.3.187
sections.original: Perdidici istaec esse vera damno cum magno meo .
sections.translation: I ’ ve thoroughly learned that this is true , to my own great misfortune .

sections.label: Section 1.3.188
sections.original: Si ecastor nunc habeas quod des , alia verba praehibeas ;
sections.translation: I ’ faith , if you now had anything to give , you ’ d be uttering different remarks ; now , since you ’ ve got nothing , you expect to be having her by means of harsh language .

sections.label: Section 1.3.190
sections.original: Non meum est .
sections.translation: ’ Tis not my way .

sections.label: Section 1.3.190b
sections.original: Nec meum quidem edepol , ad te ut mittam gratiis .
sections.translation: Nor yet mine , indeed , i ’ faith , to be sending her to you for nothing ; but this shall be done out of regard for your youthful age and your own sake , since you have rather been the cause of profit to us than of reputation to yourself . If two talents of silver [*] are paid me down , reckoned in my hand , this night will I grant you for nothing , as a present , by reason of my respect for you .

sections.label: Section 1.3.195
sections.original: Quid si non est ?
sections.translation: What if I haven ’ t it ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.195b
sections.original: Tibi non esse credam , illa alio ibit tamen .
sections.translation: I ’ ll believe that you haven ’ t it — still , she shall go to another .

sections.label: Section 1.3.196
sections.original: Vbi illaec quae dedi ante ?
sections.translation: Where is that which I have given you already ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.196b
sections.original: Abusa . nam si ea durarent mihi ,
sections.translation: Spent ; for if it was remaining to me , the damsel should be sent to you , and I should never ask for anything . Daylight , water , the sun , the moon , the night , these things I purchase not with money ; the rest , whatever we wish to enjoy , we purchase on Grecian trust [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.3.200
sections.original: quom a pistore panem petimus , vinum ex oenopolio ,
sections.translation: When we ask bread of the baker , wine from the wineshop — if they receive the money , they give their wares ; the same principle do I go upon . My hands always have eyes in them ; they believe what they see . There ’ s an old saying , trusting is good for nought [*] ; you know whose it is — I say no more .

sections.label: Section 1.3.204
sections.original: Aliam nunc mi orationem despoliato praedicas ,
sections.translation: Now I ’ m clean stripped , you tell me another tale ;

sections.label: Section 1.3.205
sections.original: longe aliam , inquam , praebes nunc atque olim , quom dabam ,
sections.translation: a very different one , I say , you give me now from formerly , when I was making presents ; a different one from formerly , when with kindness and good words you used to entice me to your house . Then did your house even smile upon me , when I used to come to you . You used to say that I alone of all loved you and her . When I had given anything , just like the young ones of a pigeon were you both upon my lips ;

sections.label: Section 1.3.210
sections.original: usque eratis , meo de studio studia erant vostra omnia ,
sections.translation: and all your likings were according to my own liking . You always kept close to me ; whatever I requested , whatever I wished , you used to do ; what I didn ’ t wish and forbade , that , with carefulness , you used to avoid , nor did you first venture to attempt to do it . Now , you jades , you don ’ t much care either what I do wish or what I don ’ t wish .

sections.label: Section 1.3.215
sections.original: Non tu scis ? hic noster quaestus aucupi simillimust .
sections.translation: Don ’ t you know ? This calling of ours is very like that of the fowler . The fowler , when he has prepared the spot , sprinkles the food about . The birds are accustomed to the spot . ’ Tis necessary for him [*] to make an outlay , who seeks for gain . They eat often ; if they are caught once , they reimburse the fowler .

sections.label: Section 1.3.219_220
sections.original: itidem hic apud nos : aedes nobis area est , auceps sum ego ,
sections.translation: So in like manner here with us . Our house is the spot , I am the fowler , the courtesan is the food , the couch is the decoy , the lovers the birds . By kindly welcoming them , by addressing them courteously , by dallying , and by chattering over the wine , and amusing conversation , they are won . If one of them has touched her bosom , that is not without advantage to the fowler . If he has taken a kiss , him you may take without a net . That you should be forgetful of these things , you who have been schooled so long !

sections.label: Section 1.3.227
sections.original: Tua ista culpa est , quae discipulum semidoctum abs te amoves .
sections.translation: That ’ s your own fault , in turning away from you a scholar half instructed .

sections.label: Section 1.3.228
sections.original: Remeato audacter , mercedem sí eris nactus : nunc abi .
sections.translation: Come back again without hesitation , if you ’ ve got the pay ; for the present , be off .

sections.label: Section 1.3.229
sections.original: Mane , mane , audi . dic , quid me aequom censes pro illa tibi dare ,
sections.translation: Stay , stay ; don ’ t you hear me ? Say what you think it fair that I should give you for her , that for this year she may be with no one else .

sections.label: Section 1.3.230b
sections.original: Tene ? viginti minas ;
sections.translation: What , you ? Twenty minae . And on this condition : if any other person shall bring them first to me , to you — good-bye .

sections.label: Section 1.3.232
sections.original: At ego est etiam prius quam abis quod volo loqui .
sections.translation: But I — There ’ s still something that I wish to say to you before you go .
